findings.description: Title/header formatting is simplified in HTML and loses the centered multi-line hierarchy seen in the PDF.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the document title is centered over multiple lines with stronger visual hierarchy; in the HTML, it appears as a left-aligned continuous heading line with reduced structural distinction.

findings.description: The HTML includes substantial fabricated wrapper content and styling not present in the PDF, including a full HTML document shell, CSS theme, Google Fonts import, and branded theme text.
findings.evidence: HTML contains text/code not present in the PDF such as "Converted Document", "Digital Commons NC — Standalone Theme CSS", "@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Source+Sans+3...')", and CSS variables/classes like "--nc-navy", ".page-header", ".page-footer". The PDF shows only the notice content and does not contain these textual/metadata elements.

findings.description: The HTML does not preserve heading structure using semantic heading tags; the document title block is represented as bold paragraphs instead of h1/h2/etc.
findings.evidence:
PDF has a clear title block: "SUMMARY OF NOTICE OF INTENT TO REDEVELOP A BROWNFIELDS PROPERTY", followed by property name and project number as heading-like lines. HTML encodes these as a single

...

block and uses no h1-h6 tags for the actual document headings.
